real_meaning: This pattern expresses genuine uncertainty — the speaker thinks something is possible but has no strong evidence either way. It conveys honest hedging: 'I'm not ruling it out, but I'm not confident either.' Unlike English 'maybe,' it carries a slightly introspective tone, as if the speaker is reasoning through possibilities out loud rather than making a guess for the listener's benefit.

formation.rule: Attaches to the plain (dictionary/short) form of verbs, i-adjectives, and na-adjectives/nouns. Verb plain form: <ruby>来<rt>く</rt></ruby>るかもしれない. I-adjective: <ruby>寒<rt>さむ</rt></ruby>いかもしれない. Na-adjective/noun: <ruby>病気<rt>びょうき</rt></ruby>かもしれない (no だ before かも). The contracted spoken form ～かもしれん or simply ～かも is extremely common in casual speech.
formation.common_mistake: Learners often insert だ before かも with na-adjectives and nouns, mirroring the pattern for plain-form statements. Wrong: <ruby>病気<rt>びょうき</rt></ruby>だかもしれない. Correct: <ruby>病気<rt>びょうき</rt></ruby>かもしれない. The だ is dropped before かも.

sentences.jp: あ、<ruby>財布<rt>さいふ</rt></ruby><ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れてきたかも。
sentences.translation: Oh, I might've forgotten my wallet.
sentences.notes: The full ～かもしれない is clipped to ～かも, which is the most natural form in casual spoken Japanese — hearing the full form here would actually sound slightly stiff.

sentences.jp: <ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby>は<ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るかもしれないので、<ruby>傘<rt>かさ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>持<rt>も</rt></ruby>っていったほうがいいですよ。
sentences.translation: It might rain tomorrow, so you'd better take an umbrella.
sentences.notes: Using かもしれない as a premise with ので shows how the pattern naturally chains with advice — the speaker isn't certain but the uncertainty itself is the reason for the suggestion.

sentences.jp: このデータには<ruby>誤<rt>ご</rt></ruby><ruby>差<rt>さ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>含<rt>ふく</rt></ruby>まれているかもしれないため、<ruby>慎重<rt>しんちょう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>解釈<rt>かいしゃく</rt></ruby>する<ruby>必要<rt>ひつよう</rt></ruby>があります。
sentences.translation: As this data may contain margin of error, careful interpretation is necessary.
sentences.notes: In formal written contexts, ～かもしれないため (because it may ~) is a standard hedging construction used in reports and academic writing to signal responsible epistemic caution.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>はもう<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>ってるかもしれないけど、やっぱり<ruby>自分<rt>じぶん</rt></ruby>から<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>うべきだと<ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>う。
sentences.translation: He might already know, but I still think you should tell him yourself.
sentences.notes: Here かもしれない modifies the premise of a moral judgment — the possibility doesn't cancel the obligation. This 'even if possibly true, the conclusion stands' use surprises learners who treat かもしれない as only a soft guess.

confused_with.contrast: Both express uncertainty, but they feel very different. ～かもしれない is genuinely open — the speaker has no strong lean, just acknowledging a possibility. ～でしょう／～だろう expresses the speaker's confident inference or expectation, often backed by reasoning or evidence. Minimal pair: <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るかもしれない means 'it might rain (I'm not sure either way),' while <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るでしょう means 'it'll probably rain (I expect it will, looking at those clouds).' Use かもしれない when you genuinely lack confidence; use でしょう when you're reasoning toward a likely conclusion.

bunpro_tip: Picture yourself shrugging and saying 'I'm not ruling it out' — that's exactly the feeling of かもしれない. When drilling, always mentally check: am I genuinely 50/50 uncertain? If yes, かもしれない. If you're leaning strongly one way based on evidence, reach for だろう instead. Also drill the casual clipped form ～かも so it feels natural, not just the full textbook form.
